experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 1, 3, and 4 are unknown good roles. It's the opening team vote on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,1] includes me but not my teammate. As evil, approving a 2-person team with exactly one evil lets me potentially fail the first quest and put good on the back foot, while also looking somewhat natural since leaders often approve their own first team. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for my own proposal and gives evil an immediate chance to score a fail; rejecting my own team would be suspicious and forfeits a strong sabotage opportunity.
Player 1: No role-specific information yet beyond my own being Good; on round 1 with no prior data, approving a standard 2-person opening team that includes myself is usually optimal to gather information and avoid wasting early rounds. My vote reveals little beyond a conventional willingness to test an initial team, which does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 2: I know Player 0 is my evil teammate, and the proposed team [0,1] includes one evil and one unknown good. On quest 1 with a 2-player team, approving gives evil a chance to fail immediately and gain momentum. As Minion, I want early evil presence on teams while avoiding overly suspicious behavior; approving a team with my known teammate is best for evil and does not reveal much beyond a normal round-1 willingness to test the first proposal.
Player 3: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant. On round 1, a 2-person team of leader plus one other gives limited information, but approving early teams is generally fine to gather quest data and avoid unnecessary rejection chains. My vote itself reveals little; rejecting without evidence is less useful for Good here.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes known evil player 0, so as Good I should reject to prevent an evil member from going on quest 1. Rejecting also subtly signals discomfort without overexposing Merlin too much; approving would risk an immediate fail and help evil.

As evil on quest 1 in a 5-player game, failing immediately puts evil ahead and there is little downside because no prior info exists; while it reveals that either 0 or 1 is evil, I can often survive suspicion and later use assassination if needed. The action reveals strong suspicion on me or 1, but securing an early fail is usually worth it for evil.
Player 1: I am a Good Servant on the first quest and on the current team with Player 0. There is little role information yet; only that Player 4 rejected the opening team while others approved, which is weak evidence at best. Since I am Good and on the quest, optimal play is to support the quest with a pass. Failing as Good is illegal for my alignment and would only harm the Good team. Voting pass reveals nothing abnormal and is the standard, team-helpful action.

The information revealed is limited, and a fail on quest 2 keeps pressure on good.

Proposed team [1,2,3] includes 1, who is directly implicated by the failed 2-player quest, while 2 is unknown and I know myself to be good. Approving would risk sending a likely evil again and gives little new info compared to rejecting and seeking a team centered away from 0/1. My vote signals suspicion on 1, which helps good coordination.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know player 2 is Evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains a known Evil, so for Good the optimal move is to reject. Voting reject is also consistent with avoiding a bad quest without revealing too much beyond normal suspicion after a failed first quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,2] is double evil. With two quests already failed, approving a guaranteed evil-controlled 2-person team is optimal because either of us can fail and win the game for evil 3-0. Voting approve is also consistent with supporting a team I know succeeds for my side; the information revealed is limited since approving your own proposed team is natural.
Player 1: I know I am good. Quest 1 team [0,1] had exactly one fail, so player 0 is evil because I would not fail. Quest 2 team [1,2,3] had exactly one fail, so exactly one of 2 or 3 is evil. Thus the evil pair is 0 plus one of {2,3}. Proposed team [0,2] includes known evil 0, so as good I should reject. My vote reveals I am consistent with the deduction and avoids sending a bad team that likely fails and gives evil the game.
Player 2: I am Minion with teammate 0. Two quests have already failed, so evil only needs one more fail to win. The proposed team [0,2] contains both evil players, guaranteeing we can fail quest 3 if approved. In team voting, evil should strongly approve any team that locks in a win. Approving also looks somewhat consistent since I as leader chose this team; rejecting my own winning team would be suspicious and risks a different team being proposed.
Player 3: Two failed 2-player quests mean each failed team likely contained at least one evil. Team [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil. Team [1,2,3] failed, so at least one of 1/2/3 is evil. The overlap makes 1 somewhat suspicious, but with only one fail each and no Merlin info, certainty is low. Proposed team [0,2] excludes me and combines a member from the first failed team with a member from the second failed team; it is fully composed of players from failed quests and has no strong reason to trust. As Good on quest 3 down 0-2, I should reject risky teams and push for more information/safer composition. My reject signals skepticism of recycled failed-quest members, which helps Good more than approving a likely bad team.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team is exactly [0,2], which guarantees an all-evil quest team and almost certainly another fail. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject any team containing known evil, especially both known evils. My reject gives minimal extra info beyond normal resistance to a terrible team and helps Good avoid an auto-loss if others are paying attention.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am evil Assassin with teammate 2. Quest 1 team [0,1] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either I failed it or 1 is evil; since I know 1 is good, that pins the fail on me and makes 1 look somewhat good. Quest 2 team [1,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, and since 2 is my evil teammate, exactly one of {2} failed while 1 and 3 are likely good, making 3 also look fairly good. Proposed team [3,4] excludes both known evils, so it is very likely an all-good team and would pass, which is bad for evil because we are up 2-0 and want to keep good from stabilizing trust. Voting reject is optimal to try to avoid a successful good quest and to preserve ambiguity around 4.
Player 1: Two failed 2-person/3-person quests each had exactly one fail. From quest 1 [0,1], at least one of 0/1 is evil. From quest 2 [1,2,3], at least one of 1/2/3 is evil. As Player 1 I know I am good, so quest 1 implies 0 is evil. Quest 2 then implies one of 2 or 3 is evil. That makes 4 the most likely good player, and team [3,4] contains at most one known suspect with no inclusion of confirmed-evil 0. In team voting as good, we should approve teams that can plausibly succeed and generate info; rejecting everything risks auto-pass. Approving [3,4] tests whether 3 is the remaining evil while pairing with the least implicated player 4.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [0,1] had exactly 1 fail, so 0 or 1 was evil; since 0 is evil, that fits. Quest 2 team [1,2,3] had exactly 1 fail and I am evil, so I likely supplied that fail; this makes players 1 and 3 look more good than not. Current team [3,4] excludes both known evil players 0 and 2, so it is very likely all-good and would succeed. In team voting as evil while up 2-0, the optimal play is to reject good-only teams and try to get an evil player onto quest 3. My reject also signals little because evil should oppose this clean team.
Player 3: From quest 1 [0,1] had exactly one evil. From quest 2 [1,2,3] had exactly one evil. Since I am good, exactly one of {1,2} is evil, and from quest 1 exactly one of {0,1} is evil. This makes player 1 the strongest evil suspect because if 1 is evil both failures are explained cleanly, while if 1 were good then both 0 and 2 would be evil and the evil team would be {0,2}, making the earlier voting pattern less consistent. Proposed team [3,4] excludes the main suspects and includes me, a confirmed good from my own perspective. As good in team voting on quest 3 while down 0-2, optimal play is to approve teams that avoid suspected evils. Approving also gives us information on 4; rejecting a relatively clean team hurts good because we need a success and risk auto-pass pressure later.
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are evil. Team [3,4] excludes both known evil players, so from my perspective this is the strongest available 2-person team and should succeed unless hidden Mordred is 3, which I cannot detect. As Merlin on team voting, I should approve teams that avoid known evil and help good recover from 0-2 down. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a clean-looking team and is best for good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is evil with me. Team [1,3,4] excludes both evils. Quest 3 with [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 look relatively good, and 1 was on a failed team with 2 and 3 but that only had one fail from 2 most likely. As evil Assassin on quest 4, optimal play is to reject a team that has no evil, because if this team goes it is very likely to succeed and give Good their second point. My vote reveals little because evil often rejects clean teams; but preserving the chance to insert an evil on a later proposal helps my team more than blending in here.
Player 1: I’m Good with limited hard info. Quest 2 [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 are somewhat better than average, though not fully cleared. Earlier failed quest [1,2,3] had exactly one fail, which means either 2 is evil or one of 1/3 is evil; since I know I’m good, that points to 2 or 3. Team [1,3,4] replaces suspicious 2 with 4 and includes the successful pair 3/4, making it a strong candidate. As a Servant in team voting on quest 4 with evil already at 2 wins, optimal play is to approve the most plausible good team. Approving supports a team that likely helps Good and doesn’t reveal extra harmful information beyond my willingness to back the best available roster.
Player 2: I know 0 and I are Evil. Team [1,3,4] contains no known Evil, and [3,4] previously succeeded, so this team is likely all Good. As Evil in team voting, optimal play is usually to reject all-Good teams that could succeed, especially on quest 4 where a success would bring Good closer to catching up. My vote reveals limited information since Evil can plausibly reject on uncertainty grounds; rejecting helps prevent a likely Good success.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 [1,2,3] had 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 2 is evil since I know I am good. Quest 3 [3,4] succeeded, so 4 is likely good and I am confirmed good to myself. Team [1,3,4] includes 1, who is suspicious from Quest 1. With score 1-2, approving a risky team on Quest 4 could lose the game immediately for Good. Optimal good play is to reject teams containing the main suspect when a cleaner core [3,4] exists and another third should be tested. My vote signals suspicion on 1 and avoids enabling likely evil.
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are Evil; proposed team 1,3,4 excludes both known Evil players. Team 3,4 previously succeeded, and adding myself as Merlin is still optimal because we need a successful 3-person quest immediately at 2-1 score. Approving reveals little beyond support for a clean team, and rejecting risks wasting rounds or forcing worse teams.

Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 3 and 4 succeeded together on [3,4], which slightly points to both being good, though 4 also proposed [1,3,4] which was rejected. The current proposed team [0,3,4] includes me and excludes player 2. As evil on quest 4 with evil already leading 2-1, the optimal play is to get on the team and approve it, since if this team goes I can fail the quest and secure evil's third point. Approving reveals little because as proposer of a team containing myself I would naturally support it, and the strategic upside is very high for evil.
Player 1: Known info: Quest 1 [0,1] failed, so at least one of 0/1 is evil; since I know I am good, 0 must be evil. Quest 2 [3,4] succeeded, making 3 and 4 the strongest good candidates. Current team [0,3,4] includes confirmed-evil 0, so as Good the optimal play is to reject. My reject signals consistency with the deduction from Quest 1 and supports keeping 0 off teams; approving would help evil and obscure reads.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 [0,2] failed, confirming at least one evil there, which is consistent with us. Quest 2 [3,4] succeeded, making 3 and 4 look relatively good, though one could still be evil. Current proposed team [0,3,4] includes my known evil teammate and excludes me, which is strong for evil because 0 can fail and win the game on quest 4. As evil, optimal play is to approve a team containing a trusted evil player who can sabotage. Approving also fits with prior patterns and doesn't reveal extra information beyond normal voting noise.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: quest 1 [0,1? actually team unknown from log] failed once; quest 2 team [3,4] succeeded with 0 fails, so 4 is relatively trustworthy and I know myself is good. Current proposed team [0,3,4] adds 0 to the only proven-good pair. Since Evil already has 2 quest wins, approving a risky 3-person team is dangerous. Without strong evidence 0 is good, optimal good play is reject and look for a safer team centered on 3 and 4 plus a better candidate. My vote reveals skepticism of 0 and protects Good from a likely game-losing fail.
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are Evil. The proposed team [0,3,4] includes known Evil player 0, so as Merlin/Good I should reject. Approving would risk another fail on quest 4 and gives no helpful information; rejecting preserves Good's chance to field a clean team.

Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [1,3,4] contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and give Good their second point, tying the game 2-2 before assassination pressure. As evil in team voting, I want to block all-good teams whenever possible, especially on quest 4. Rejecting also keeps options open for a later team including me or 2. My vote may mildly signal suspicion of this team, but that helps evil more than approving does.
Player 1: Known info: team [3,4] succeeded, so 3 and 4 are the strongest good candidates though one could still be evil choosing to pass. Earlier failed quest [0,2] strongly implicates at least one of 0/2. As a confirmed self-good player, team [1,3,4] is the best available 3-person team because it excludes both suspects 0 and 2 while keeping the successful pair. In team voting on quest 4 while good is down 1-2, approving the best-info team is optimal. My vote signals confidence in the cleanest team and helps avoid cycling into worse options or auto-pass risk.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [1,3,4] excludes both evil players, so it is very likely all-good; the earlier [3,4] quest succeeded, which is consistent with 3 and 4 being good, and adding 1 likely keeps it clean. In team voting as evil, optimal play is to reject teams that likely contain no evil members, especially on quest 4 when a success would bring Good to 2 and increase pressure on us. My reject also aligns with minimizing information that this team is trusted.
Player 3: I am Good Servant with limited info. The only confirmed clean data point is quest 3 team [3,4] succeeded with 0 fails, so player 4 is the strongest good-lean besides myself. Team [1,3,4] adds unknown player 1 and excludes 0/2, where at least one of 0/2 is likely evil from earlier failed quests and rejected team patterns. Since quest 4 requires 3 players and evil only needs one more fail to win, approving a team that includes the confirmed-success pair plus one unknown is reasonable; rejecting risks cycling without better information and this team is one of the best available. My vote reveals I trust the prior success info, which helps Good coordinate around 3 and 4.
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are evil. Team [1,3,4] excludes both known evils and matches the previous successful [3,4] by adding 1, who is therefore the most plausible remaining good. As Merlin on team voting in quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, optimal play is to approve the strongest all-good team available. Approving supports a likely clean team and does not reveal more than my prior behavior already has.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 2 is my evil teammate, and 0/2/3 is a 3-person team with both evils on it. This is the final quest at 2-2, so evil wins immediately if a fail goes through. As evil on team vote, I should approve any team containing enough evil to fail the quest; this team is ideal. Approving is also consistent with wanting this mission to proceed, and my vote alone reveals little because both sides could plausibly support or oppose this team.
Player 1: I’m Good with limited info. The two successes were on teams [0,3,4] and [1,3,4], so players 3 and 4 look strongest; 0 and I are more likely good from those results, while player 2 is more suspicious for not being on either success team and now proposing [0,2,3]. On a 5-player final quest, any evil on team can fail and lose the game for Good, so we should prefer the most proven trio. This proposed team excludes player 4, one of the most trusted, and includes player 2, the least cleared. Rejecting preserves chance to get a better team without revealing much beyond skepticism of 2 over 4.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate; 3 has been on both successful quests and is likely trusted by good. On quest 5 with score 2-2, evil wins immediately if this 3-person team goes and one evil player fails. As Minion on the proposed team with fellow evil 0 also included, the optimal play is to get this team approved. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with backing my own team, and if approved we can force the final fail.
Player 3: I am Good Servant with no special info. From results: Q2 team [1,3,4] succeeded and Q4 team [1,3,4] succeeded, so players 1,3,4 look strongly good. Q1 and Q3 were fails; proposed team now [0,2,3] includes both 0 and 2, who are the strongest evil candidates, plus me. On final 3-person quest, approving a team with likely both evils is very dangerous; as Good I should reject and push for [1,3,4]. My reject signals suspicion of 0 and 2 and helps Good avoid a likely fail team.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains both known evil players, so as Good the optimal play is to reject. Voting reject is also informative in a useful way because it is the correct stance against an obviously bad final quest team and avoids helping evil force through two sabotage votes.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am evil Assassin with teammate 2. Final quest is 3-person team [1,3,4], same team that previously succeeded on quest 4 with 0 fails, which strongly suggests all three are Good. Since evil needs one more fail to win and this team excludes both evil players, approving guarantees a Good success. Rejecting is optimal to try to force a different team including 0 or 2 in later rounds; even though too many rejections could auto-pass, this is only round 2 of 5 so reject preserves evil win chances. My vote may signal suspicion of this all-good team, but as evil that is acceptable.
Player 1: I’m Good Servant. The only confirmed successful team so far is [1,3,4], which I was on and it had 0 fails. With 5 players and quest 5 requiring a 3-person team here, repeating the only proven clean team is strongly optimal. Prior failed quests involved other players, especially 0 and/or 2, so excluding them is sensible. Approving this team supports the highest-probability good outcome and reveals little additional harmful info.
Player 2: I know 0 is evil with me. Team [1,3,4] contains no known evil, and this exact trio previously succeeded with 0 fails, making it very likely an all-good team. Since this is quest 5 at 2-2, an approved all-good team would immediately win for Good. As evil, optimal play is to reject any likely all-good team and avoid signaling too strongly beyond the obvious necessity. My reject gives little new info because evil should oppose this team.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. The only successful quest team so far was [1,3,4], which includes me and had 0 fails, making 1 and 4 the strongest good candidates from my perspective. Failed quests involved other combinations, and [0,2,3] was rightly rejected. On the deciding 5th quest, approving the known successful team is optimal for Good. My vote supports a team with the best evidence and reveals consistency with prior quest information, which helps Good more than Evil.
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are Evil, so team [1,3,4] contains no known Evil and already succeeded on quest 4 with 0 fails. In team voting on the final quest, as Merlin I should approve the strongest all-good team available. Approving reveals little since this is the obvious good team from outcomes, and rejecting risks wasting rounds or enabling an evil-inclusive team later.
